item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but the overall threat of the object does not go over very well. It goes from shrapnel to destroying an entire foundation base in a single sentence and none of the rest of the article hints at that level of threat, as the foundation is usually this thorough for every anomaly. So basically what I'm trying to say is that the end note about how HUNDREDS OF THOUSANDS WILL DIE FROM A VERY OBSCURE MISTAKE at first makes no sense. I get where you're coming from, with the whole "one dog toy destroyed an entire foundation site" but 98% of the rest of the article gives off the "it's just another random object malfunctioning and burning the house down" or whatever. I did like the "WHY WON'T THE CHILDREN WAKE UP, THEY NEED TO GET TO SCHOOL" part because it both lightens the mood and clarifies it's not some ghost mother trapped in the ratings section or whatever.

TL;DR,

one star, i thought i was getting a regular scp but the part that dignified it as keter is about 2 lines, thus making that designation (and the ending note) feel out of place, which killed my whole family and their kids, why won't they wake up I have a meeting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this SCP but I do believe it could be expanded upon by foundation researchers trying to source the anomaly via IP addresses and the like, obviously unsuccessfully, but it would be the obvious route for them to take and it almost feels wrong to see no mention of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate that feedback. Let me see if I can build a stronger ending note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this is at all related to Totelighsoft "BECAUSE COMPUTERS!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test logs are nice, but the last note is weak. Is the Foundation too incompetent to understand an algorithmic bot? The description is overdone as well, but I think I could easily be convinced to swap my vote if the main description was more polished and the ending changed to make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oof, that's a typo! It's meant to say 187. My bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Agent said. Good concept and good execution, but there are few (very minor) grammatical issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but "387 decibels" is a problem.

60 decibels is twice as loud as 50 decibels.

It's good that you realize that there would be "catastrophic architectural failure due to damage by sonic waves (… with) D-Class personnel up to 900 meters away suffer(ing) tissue ruptures and cranial bleeding that was fatal within minutes", except that it's more along the lines of "the planet was destroyed".

194 dB is the loudest sound possible on the surface of the Earth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I liked the SCP, but I would have appreciated if there was an answer for their question, or some kind of followup. It doesn't feel like it contributes anything that couldn't be explained more succinctly elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The escalation here is pretty humorous, and I'm usually pretty receptive to SCPs about real-world phenomena. Narratively, though, what's the purpose of the second addendum? It seems to just be re-emphasizing the threat the anomaly presents when that was already made clear in the first addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! If you can share some of the formatting mistakes I missed, I'm happy to fix 'em.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very interesting concept! I liked it very much… it could have been more polished, for sure, and there were some occasional formatting mistakes but overall… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If rewritten, could've potentially worked as a joke SCP.
